title: Rambhuwan vs Lallan Prasad on 7 May, 2026
body: Rambhuwan vs Lallan Prasad on 7 May, 2026 Author: Maninder S. Bhatti Bench: Maninder S. Bhatti NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 1 FA-26-2006 IN THE HIGH COURT OF MADHYA PRADESH AT JABALPUR BEFORE HON'BLE SHRI JUSTICE MANINDER S. BHATTI ON THE 7 th OF MAY, 2026 FIRST APPEAL No. 26 of 2006 RAMBHUWAN Versus LALLAN PRASAD AND OTHERS Appearance: Shri Sarvesh Kumar Jaiswal - Advocate for the appellant. None for the respondents, though all the respondents are served. ORDER This First Appeal has been filed under Section 96 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 by the plaintiff/appellant against the judgment and decree dated 08.11.2005 passed by the Additional District Judge, Panna in Civil Suit No.27-A/2004, whereby the suit of the appellant/plaintiff was dismissed by the trial Court. 2. The brief facts of the case are that the appellant/plaintiff had filed a Civil Suit for permanent injunction and declaration of registered sale deed dated 14.05.2004 and 05.06.2004 in respect of disputed land bearing area 1.11 (18 Acres 96 dhismil) Khasra no. 54 of Mauja Fuldari, Tehsil and District Panna. An oral partition had taken place amongst the family members about 15 years prior to the institution of the suit. Since then, the parties have been in separate possession of their respective shares. Subsequently, incorrect revenue entries were recorded in the name of Hetram Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 2 FA-26-2006 and thereafter in the names of his descendants, despite the plaintiff/appellant being in possession of the land in question. Allegedly, respondent No.1, having no right, over the property in question, illegally executed the impugned sale deeds in favour of third parties. Aggrieved thereby, the present appellant/plaintiff filed a suit before the trial Court, which vide impugned ex-parte judgment and decree dated 08.11.2005 has been dismissed. Ultimately, the appellant has approached this Court through this first appeal. 3. Learned counsel for the appellant/plaintiff contends that it is a case where the Trial Court has failed to appreciate that there were specific pleadings as well as evidence to the effect that the land in question originally belonged to one Shaligdeen and in paragraph 3 of the memorandum of plaint, a family tree was depicted. As per the said family tree, the appellant/plaintiff was in possession of the property in question along with other family members. The said averments so made in paragraph 3 of the plaint, were also admitted by defendants/respondent No.4 to 6. The aforesaid defendants filed written statements and admitted the contents of the memorandum of plaint. The remaining defendants did not enter their appearance and ultimately, even after filing of the written statement by defendants No. 4 to 6, all the defendants were proceeded ex-parte by the trial Court. The trial Court, thus was obliged to appreciate that in the absence of any rebuttal to the pleadings as well as any contrary evidence thereto, the claim of the present appellant could not have been turned down. It is contended by counsel for the appellant that the trial Court has held that no Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 3 FA-26-2006 benefit could have been accrued in favour of the appellant on the strength of an order passed by the Revenue Authority in purported exercise of powers conferred under Sections 115 / 116 of the Madhya Pradesh Land Revenue Code, 1959 (hereinafter referred to as 'the MPLRC'). 4. It is further contended by the counsel that, according to the trial Court, Sections 115 and 116 of the MPLRC could not have been invoked in order to mutate the name of any person, on the contrary, the said statutory provisions were confined to the extent of correction of any clerical error in the revenue records. It is contended by the counsel that the findings as arrived in paragraph 12 of the judgment of the trial Court, are misconceived a n d , prima facie, go contrary to the statutory provisions contained in Sections 115 and 116 of the MPLRC. It is further contended by the counsel that in the case in hand, the Tehsildar passed an order dated 29.03.2000 (Exhibit P/12), a perusal of which reflects that the factum of possession of the present appellant over the property in question was taken note of and directions were issued to record the factum of the possession of the present appellant in the revenue records. Accordingly, the orders contained in Exhibits P/13 and P/14 were also passed. The aforesaid orders could not have been treated to be orders without jurisdiction, inasmuch as the statutory provisions contained in Sections 115 and 116 of the MPLRC conferred the power upon the Revenue Authority to recognize the factum of possession of a person over a particular Khasra and thus the said order, since were not assailed before any other Appellate Authority and having attained finality, their validity could not have been disputed by the trial Court while passing Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 4 FA-26-2006 the impugned judgment. 5. It is the further contention of the counsel for the appellant/plaintiff that the trial Court grossly erred in holding that the factum of oral partition between the family members of the present appellant/plaintiff was not proved, which, according to plaint was carried out somewhere in the year 1977-1978. It is contended by the counsel that the trial Court was required to appreciate that defendant Nos. 4 to 6, who are brothers of the present appellant/plaintiff, have admitted the factum of partition in the return filed on their behalf. Moreover, there were unrebutted testimonies of all the plaintiff witnesses, including the plaintiff/appellant himself. Even the independent witnesses supported the factum of partition almost 25-26 years back. There are evidence of independent witnesses, namely, Basanta Chamar (PW 2), Swamideen (PW 3) and Soukhi Lal (PW 4). If the testimonies of the aforesaid witnesses remained uncontroverted, the trial Court in the absence of any contradictory material, could not have concluded that there was a failure on the part of the present appellant/plaintiff to establish that there was an oral partition in the year 1977-1978. It is thus contended by the counsel that the impugned judgment deserves to be set aside. 6. In the case in hand, the respondents have been served but there is no appearance on behalf of the respondents. It is also undisputed that the respondents/defendants before the trial Court were also eventually proceeded ex-parte. 7. No other point is expressed or argued by the counsel for the Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 5 FA-26-2006 appellant. 8. Heard the submission advanced on behalf of the appellant and perused the records. 9. A perusal of the record reflects that the trial Court has declined to entertain the suit filed by the appellant/plaintiff on the ground that an order passed by Revenue Authority under Sections 115 / 116 of MPLRC, since were unsustainable, no right on the basis of the said order could have been claimed by the present appellant/plaintiff. The trial Court has also declined to entertain the claim on the ground that there was a failure on the part of the present appellant/plaintiff to prove that a partition underwent in the year 1977-1978. 10. To deal with the aforesaid findings, it is first germane to deal with the orders passed by the Revenue Authority, which are contained in Exhibits P/12 to P/14. A perusal of the aforesaid reflects that the appellant/plaintiff had approached the Revenue Authority with a prayer that he was in possession of the property in question and therefore his name was required to be mutated in the revenue records. The applications were moved by the present appellant/plaintiff under Sections 115 and 116 of MPLRC. Section 116 of the MPLRC has been omitted subsequently by the legislature vide M.P. Act No.23 of 2018. which came into force from 25.09.2018. However, before the omission and substitution of Section 116 of the MPLRC, earlier provisions of Sections 115 and 116 were holding the field. The aforementioned relevant provisions of the MPLRC are reproduced as under: "115. Correction of wrong entry in khasra and any other land records by superior officers- If any Tasildar finds that a wrong or Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 6 FA-26-2006 incorrect entry has been made in the land records prepared under Section 114 by an officer sub-ordinate to him, he shall direct necessary changes to be made therein in red ink after making such enquiry from the person concerned as he may deem fit after due written notice. "116. Disputes regarding entry in khasra or in any other land records- (1) If any person is aggrieved by an entry made in the land records prepared under Section 114 in respect of matters other than those referred to in Section 108 , he shall apply to the Tahsildar for its correction within one year of the date of such entry. (2) The Tahsildar shall, after making such enquiry as he may deem fit, pass necessary orders in the matter." 11. A perusal of the aforesaid reflects that if the Tehsildar finds that a wrong or incorrect entry has been made in the land records prepared under Section 114 of the MPLRC by any Officer subordinate to him, he shall direct necessary changes to be made therein in red ink, after making inquiry from the person concerned, as he may deem fit after the written notice. Section 116 of the MPLRC provides for the disposal of disputes pertaining to entries in Khasra or in any other land record. 12. As per the aforesaid provisions, if any person is aggrieved by an entry made in any land record prepared under Section 114 of the MPLRC, he shall apply to the Tehsildar for its correction within one year from the date of such entry. Thereupon, the Tehsildar after making such inquiry as he deems fit, shall pass a necessary order in that regard. A collective reading of both provisions reflect that a Revenue Authority can be approached seeking correction in the revenue records. However, the legislature in its own wisdom has fixed a time span to ventilate grievance by the person aggrieved Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 7 FA-26-2006 by the entry thereof. A perusal of the orders contained in Exhibits P/12 to P/14 reveal that the Revenue Authority did not appreciate the said statutory provisions contained in Section 116 of the MPLRC. The said section provided that an application for correction could have been made within one year from the date of the entry, which was being sought to be disputed by the person concerned. 13. The entire orders which are contained in Exhibit P/12 and P/13 nowhere reflect that it was the case of the present appellant/plaintiff that he was seeking correction in the revenue record within one year from the entries which were made in the revenue record. Even otherwise, according to appellant/plaintiff's own case, Exhibit P/6 i.e. Khasra Panchshala was produced before the trial Court and the said Khasra Panchshala specifically included the name of respondent No.1 in respect of Khasra in dispute and it was also specifically mentioned that there was a standing wheat crop. Therefore, apparently as per the documents produced by the appellant/plaintiff himself, the name of defendant No.1 was duly recorded in the revenue entries and accordingly considering the factum of recording of revenue entries in Exhibit P/6, the trial Court concluded that in the year 1996, the land in question was recorded in the name of defendant No.1. There was no entry in Exhibit P/6 as regards the present appellant/plaintiff. The appellant/plaintiff produced the Khasra Panchshala of 2003-2004 as Exhibit P/9 and in the said Khasra also the name of defendant No.1 was recorded as land owner. 14. The trial Court has also considered the aspect that the name of the Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 8 FA-26-2006 appellant/plaintiff was never recorded prior to the year 1999-2000, in the revenue records nor there was any document on record to establish that there was a partition of the property in the year 1977-1978. Accordingly, the trial Court came to a conclusion that in absence of any evidence so as to substantiate the factum of partition in the year 1977-1978, no relief could have been extended in favour of the appellant/plaintiff. The trial Court had observed in paragraph 12 that the Tehsildar while passing the orders contained in Exhibit P/12 to P/14, exceeded the jurisdiction which is conferred upon him under Sections 115 and 116 of the MPLRC as the jurisdiction could have been exercised in respect of any clerical error. P er se , though such findings go contrary to the statutory provisions contained in Section 116 of the MPLRC. However, even otherwise the order of the Tehsildar which has been disputed by the trial Court, cannot be given a stamp of approval as there was total failure on the part of the Tehsildar to appreciate that any application under Section 116 of the MPLRC could have been made within one year from the date of entry in the revenue records which was being sought to be disputed by the person aggrieved. 15. Undisputedly, in the case in hand, the entries in favour of the defendant No.1 were recorded in the year 1996 also according to Exhibit P/6. Therefore, any correction of entry was permissible within the span of one year. It is also not mentioned in the orders contained in Exhibits P/12 to P/13 that the present appellant/plaintiff was unaware about entries which were recorded in the name of defendant No.1. 16. Thus, in view of the aforesaid detailed analysis, as the order passed Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2026:MPHC-JBP:36133 9 FA-26-2006 by the Tehsildar under Sections 115 and 116 of the MPLRC was even otherwise invalid, and there was failure on the part of the present appellant/plaintiff to prove the oral partition, which, according to the appellant/plaintiff took place in the year 1977-1978, the trial Court did not commit any error while passing the impugned judgement dated 08.11.2005 in Civil Suit No.27-A/2004. 17. Resultantly, there exists no material to interfere with the impugned judgment as the same does not suffer from any infirmity or perversity. Accordingly, the First Appeal stands dismissed. (MANINDER S. BHATTI) JUDGE sp Signature Not Verified Signed by: SAVITRI PATEL Signing time: 12-05-2026 18:36:32
